channel_name: No Priors · Elad Gil & Sarah Guo
video_title: Travel Through the Lens of AI with with Booking.com CEO Glenn Fogel
gist: Booking Holdings CEO Glenn Fogel argues there is no permanent moat in travel, walks through how the agentic AI tool Penny is already changing conversion, cancellation and customer-service economics, reframes the OpenAI travel-checkout episode as overreaction on both sides, and lays out why scale, inventory depth and global travel regulation remain durable advantages even as he warns AI's job-destruction speed is outrunning job creation.

insights.claim: Priceline's market cap collapsed from roughly $15 billion shortly after its IPO to a couple hundred million dollars within about a year during the dot-com bust, forcing a reverse stock split to avoid delisting, before compounding into what is now Booking Holdings, a company that approached $180 billion in market cap and neared $6,000 a share (post-split) over the following quarter-century.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A concrete base rate for how a near-death dot-com survivor can compound roughly 1,000x over 25 years, useful context when judging which AI-era companies might follow a similar path versus the ~98% that historically don't.
insights.quote: And in last summer we came very close to $6,000.

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel says there is no durable competitive moat in travel and that Booking Holdings' current advantages could disappear, so the only sustainable strategy is continuously building new services rather than defending an existing position.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Signals that Booking's management does not believe in resting on incumbency advantages, implying continued heavy reinvestment in product/AI rather than harvesting cash flow, relevant to margin and reinvestment-rate expectations.
insights.quote: There is no such thing as a moat. There is no such thing as somewhere you're going to be protected against innovation.

themes.theme: AI Boom vs. Dot-Com Bubble Parallels

insights.claim: As of this conversation, OpenAI and Anthropic are rumored to have $30-50 billion in annual revenue run rate each, cited by the host as evidence some AI companies already have enormous revenue bases even as many highly valued AI startups have little or no revenue.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Useful (if unconfirmed) revenue-scale reference point for benchmarking private AI-lab valuations against actual run-rate revenue.
insights.quote: OpenAI and Anthropic are rumored to have 30 to $50 billion in revenue run rate each.

insights.claim: The host frames the current AI company boom against the 1999-2000 internet IPO cohort, noting roughly 1,500 companies went public in that window and only about two dozen still exist today, implying a similarly steep attrition rate could apply to today's AI-native startups.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A historical mortality-rate anchor (roughly 98% attrition among a speculative IPO cohort) investors can use to size expected survivorship among today's AI companies.
insights.quote: So you had 1500 companies, of which what two dozen are left at most?

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel explicitly declines to predict what fraction of today's AI companies will survive versus the dot-com era, framing speculative technology booms (citing the California Gold Rush and the Detroit auto boom) as a recurring 150-year pattern that always produces both real, lasting companies and heavy investor losses.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Reinforces a base-rate mental model: expect both genuine value creation and large capital destruction simultaneously in the AI boom, without assuming this cycle is uniquely different.

insights.claim: Booking stock fell when new AI models were seen as enabling superior agentic commerce that could disintermediate travel companies, then rose about 8% when OpenAI announced it would not act as merchant of record or keep in-app commerce for travel bookings inside ChatGPT.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Shows the market has already round-tripped a real repricing event on travel-intermediary disintermediation risk tied to a single AI platform's product decision, a signal to watch for repeat volatility around future agentic-commerce announcements from OpenAI, Google, or Anthropic.
insights.quote: when uh open AI said we're not planning to be a merchant of record trying to or we're not even going to keep in this app type of way of doing commerce. We're not doing that.

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel views neither the initial market fear that AI would eliminate travel intermediaries nor the subsequent relief rally after OpenAI's retreat as a definitive verdict on AI's impact on travel bookings.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Suggests investors should discount single-headline swings in travel-intermediary stocks tied to any one AI platform's product announcements as noisy rather than structural.
insights.quote: I wouldn't read too much into that one way or the other.

themes.theme: Penny: Booking's Agentic AI in Production

insights.claim: Adoption of Penny, Priceline's agentic AI travel-planning tool, has doubled every month for several consecutive months per the company's internal data cited on a prior earnings call.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A concrete, fast-growing usage metric for an in-production consumer AI agent inside a large incumbent, useful for tracking real agentic-commerce adoption curves rather than hype.

insights.claim: Priceline's management links Penny adoption to a measurable lift in conversion, faster search-to-booking paths, lower cancellation rates, and higher customer satisfaction/success.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Gives investors specific KPIs (conversion, cancellation rate, time-to-book) to watch as leading indicators of AI-agent monetization inside travel bookings.

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel describes personally using Priceline's Penny to plan a complex multi-city family trip with mixed cabin classes, split payment (frequent-flyer miles versus cash), and next-day city-to-city logistics, arguing agentic AI already handles trip complexity better than manual planning.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A first-hand product demo from the CEO signals genuine internal conviction in the tool's current capability, not just a marketing narrative.
insights.quote: It was wonderful. Wonderful. And that's what we want even more.

insights.claim: Booking Holdings' customer-service cost per contact is down roughly 10% due to AI handling, with customer satisfaction rising at the same time, though the company still preserves a human-agent option for customers who want it.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A tangible near-term AI-driven margin lever (customer service cost) already showing up in reported metrics, relevant to opex/margin models for online travel agencies adopting AI support.
insights.quote: per reservation looking about 10%.

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel says Booking Holdings still lacks clarity on the true unit economics of its AI agents, including per-booking token cost, how many back-and-forth exchanges are required, and the longer-term customer lifetime value and loyalty impact of AI-assisted trips.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Highlights that even leading real-world AI-agent deployments haven't yet solved token-cost-to-ROI measurement, a reminder that reported AI cost savings may understate the full unit-economics picture still being worked out industry-wide.
insights.quote: these are things that we don't know yet that we're going to have to continue to work on develop so we know

insights.claim: Booking Holdings is investing approximately $700 million this year across multiple initiatives including but not limited to AI/tech enablement, following a prior disclosure of roughly $550 million of cost savings being redirected into AI and platform investment.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of Booking's reinvestment commitment tied to AI-era transformation, a reference point for peer online-travel and platform companies' AI capex intensity.
insights.quote: We talked about 700 million uh approximately this year is being invested

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel's capital allocation rule is to reinvest in the business or pursue acquisitions only when the expected ROI clearly justifies it, and otherwise return cash to shareholders because they can redeploy it more efficiently than the company can.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A clear, reusable capital-allocation heuristic (ROI-gated reinvestment, else return capital) investors can apply when assessing management quality at other large free-cash-flow generators.
insights.quote: if you can't do either of those then get the money back to the shareholders because they can then invest it better than you can.

insights.claim: Booking Holdings has bought back approximately 40% of its outstanding shares over roughly the past twelve years, including $3.6 billion in buybacks in a single recent quarter, about $300 million in dividends, and another roughly $300 million used to cover tax withholding on vested equity grants.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Demonstrates an aggressive, sustained shareholder-return program at scale, relevant to modeling share-count trajectory and total shareholder yield for Booking Holdings.
insights.quote: we've bought back approximately 40% of the outstanding shares

themes.theme: Durable Moats: Scale, Inventory and Regulation

insights.claim: Booking Holdings closed 2025 with 8.6 million alternative-accommodation listings and, by total transactions and room nights in that category alone, is approximately three-quarters the size of Airbnb, a scale Fogel says is underappreciated, especially by U.S. observers, before even counting Booking's larger hotel business.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Reframes Airbnb's perceived category dominance: Booking Holdings is a much closer competitor in alternative accommodations globally than commonly assumed in the U.S., relevant to relative competitive positioning between the two.
insights.quote: we are approximately three quarters the size of Airbnb

insights.claim: Booking Holdings' alternative-accommodation business has grown faster than Airbnb's over the past five years, per Glenn Fogel.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A relative growth-rate data point suggesting Booking is gaining share against Airbnb in alternative accommodations, worth tracking in future disclosures.
insights.quote: we've been growing faster the last 5 years

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel argues that global travel regulation is increasingly complex, especially outside the U.S., and that this regulatory burden favors large incumbents who can afford compliance, making it much harder than it looks for an AI-native startup to simply build a slicker interface and disintermediate established travel platforms.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Identifies regulatory compliance burden as an underappreciated structural moat for scaled travel platforms, a factor to weigh against the narrative that AI agents make travel intermediaries easily replaceable.
insights.quote: if you think you're just going to come in and do this business and knock away these very big players, I say you should really understand what the business is before you decide to commit your capital.

themes.theme: AI and the Speed of Labor Disruption

insights.claim: Booking.com's shift from human translators and multilingual customer-service staff (covering 40+ languages) to machine translation eliminated that entire job category within the company, offering a concrete, already-completed example of AI-driven labor displacement rather than a hypothetical.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A real, company-specific case study of full job-category elimination from AI (machine translation), useful as a template for estimating similar displacement risk in other language- or content-heavy service roles.
insights.quote: now we have machine translations all those jobs are gone there's nobody that that's all those jobs disappear

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel's core concern about AI and jobs is not that technology displaces labor, which he views as a normal recurring historical pattern, but that the current speed of job destruction may be outpacing the speed of new job creation.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-jobs debate away from whether displacement happens toward whether the transition speed itself creates social and economic risk, a distinction relevant to policy and labor-market forecasting.

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel is most concerned about older, lower-skill workers with limited retraining options, using the example of a 50-something truck driver whose job disappears to automation, and is skeptical that government retraining programs have historically worked well.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Signals corporate leadership skepticism toward government-led retraining as a solution, suggesting private-sector upskilling initiatives may be viewed as a more credible mitigation path than public programs.
insights.quote: retraining by governments over the last 50 years really hasn't worked out so well.

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel warns that societies which reject AI adoption out of fear over job losses risk falling behind countries like China, which he says are not slowing AI adoption for those reasons.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: Frames domestic AI-adoption hesitancy driven by labor-displacement fears as a potential competitiveness risk versus China, relevant to the AI policy and national-competitiveness debate.
insights.quote: I assure you in China they're they're not having that same thing about oh AI is bad and we shouldn't do it.

insights.claim: Booking Holdings' internal response to AI-driven displacement risk is an active upskilling and 'AI literacy' program across its roughly 25,000 employees, intended to make staff more redeployable even where specific roles are automated.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A concrete example of large-company workforce strategy under AI automation that other employers and investors assessing labor-transition risk at scale can benchmark against.
insights.quote: How can we get people so they are ready for the future? How do we get them so they are become AI literate?

insights.claim: The host notes that consumer sentiment surveys on AI flip from strongly positive to strongly negative purely based on question framing: usage/benefit-framed questions produce enthusiasm, while job-loss-framed questions produce fear, implying public AI sentiment data is highly framing-dependent rather than a stable signal.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Cautions investors and policymakers against taking single-question AI sentiment polls at face value, since the same population can appear pro- or anti-AI depending on wording alone.
insights.quote: If you ask people, do you love, you know, using Chat GBT and Gemini and Claude and all the they're like, we love it and we'll pay more for it

insights.claim: Glenn Fogel declines to give a general rule for when a founder or CEO should sell versus keep building, arguing the right answer depends entirely on management's confidence in the opportunity, the specific situation, and whether the founder's underlying motivation is building something meaningful versus simply making money.
insights.speaker: Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings
insights.so_what: A practical heuristic for founders and investors evaluating exit timing: interrogate motivation and situational confidence rather than applying a blanket rule, especially relevant given today's abundance of AI-startup acquisition offers.
insights.quote: What are you really trying to do? Are you trying to accomplish something? The goal is to actually make something that matters or are you just here to make money?
